name: Paniyaram
description: Kuzhi Paniyaram is a popular South Indian breakfast and snack made from fermented rice and urad dal batter, seasoned with tempered onions, chillies, ginger and curry leaves, then cooked in a special paniyaram pan with half-round moulds. It has a crispy golden exterior and a soft, spongy interior. Known as Paddu in Karnataka and Guliyappa in Andhra, it is a great way to use leftover idli-dosa batter.

steps.instruction: Heat 2 tsp oil in a pan. Add mustard seeds and let them splutter.
Add urad dal and chana dal. Fry till golden brown.
Add green chilli, ginger, curry leaves and asafoetida. Sauté for a few seconds.
Add chopped onion and required salt. Sauté until onions turn transparent.
Add this tempering to 2 cups of idli dosa batter.
Add chopped coriander leaves. Mix well.
Add water if needed — the batter should be semi-thick, not too runny.
steps.tip: The batter should be semi-thick — too thin and paniyarams will be sticky inside; too thick and they will be dense.

steps.instruction: Heat a paniyaram pan (appe pan) on medium heat. Add ¼ tsp oil in each hole.
steps.tip: A cast iron paniyaram pan gives the best crispy exterior.

steps.instruction: Swirl the pan so oil coats all sides of each cavity evenly.
steps.tip: Swirl the pan after adding oil so all cavities are evenly coated — this prevents sticking.

steps.instruction: Fill each hole with batter up to ¾th — the paniyarams will rise while cooking.
Cook covered on low-medium heat for 3 to 5 minutes until the top looks cooked.
steps.tip: Cook covered first so the top steams and cooks through before flipping.

steps.instruction: Open the lid. When the top looks set and no longer wet, flip each paniyaram using a paniyaram turner or spoon.
steps.tip: Use a toothpick to check doneness — it should come out clean without batter sticking.

steps.instruction: Cook the other side for 2 minutes until golden brown.
Remove and transfer to a plate. Repeat with the remaining batter.
Serve hot with coconut chutney, tomato chutney or sambar.
steps.tip: Paniyarams are best eaten hot. Leftover batter can be refrigerated and used later.

tips: The batter should be semi-thick — too thin and paniyarams will be sticky inside; too thick and they will be dense.
Swirl the pan after adding oil so all cavities are evenly coated — this prevents sticking.
Cook covered first so the top steams and cooks through before flipping.
Use a toothpick to check doneness — it should come out clean without batter sticking.
A cast iron paniyaram pan gives the best crispy exterior.
You can add finely chopped carrots, capsicum or grated coconut to the batter for variation.
For sweet paniyaram, add jaggery syrup, cardamom and coconut to the batter instead of the savoury tempering.
Paniyarams are best eaten hot. Leftover batter can be refrigerated and used later.
